items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.correct: Information the performer senses from their own body during/after movement.
items.why: Students reverse the meaning. Intrinsic = from WITHIN (kinesthetic awareness, feel); extrinsic = from OUTSIDE (coach feedback, video, statistics). Experienced performers rely more on intrinsic feedback.

items.q: How long does the ATP-PC system last at maximum intensity?
items.why: Students overestimate the ATP-PC system. It provides instant energy but stores deplete in ~10 seconds (one sprint, one heavy lift). After that, glycolysis or aerobic systems take over.

items.q: How many cervical vertebrae are there?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 7. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Bone adaptation to load. “Lung volumes” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Total vertebrae in the human spine?
items.why: Exact answer: 33 vertebrae. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
